name: 21_Iris_Classification_SVM_Iris_ML
query: I request a project to classify iris species utilizing the Iris dataset with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier implemented in `src/model.py`. The project should standardize the data in and perform feature selection in `src/data_loader.py`. It will document the classification accuracy and save it as `results/metrics/classification_accuracy.txt`, and generate and save a confusion matrix as `results/figures/confusion_matrix.png`. It will further create an interactive web application in `src/app.py` using Streamlit to showcase classification results and model performance, with the figures stored in `results/figures/`. The web page should be user-friendly, with a brief explanation of the model to help users understand how the SVM classifier works.

requirements.criteria: Data is standardized to ensure feature values are within the same range in `src/data_loader.py`.
Feature selection is performed to identify important features in `src/data_loader.py`.
A confusion matrix is generated and saved as `results/figures/confusion_matrix.png`.
An interactive web application `src/app.py` is created using "Streamlit"` to showcase classification results and model performance in results/figures/.

preferences.criteria: The Streamlit web page should be user-friendly, allowing users to easily explore different aspects of the model's performance.
A brief model explanation should be included on the web page, helping users understand how the SVM classifier works.

reason:
<UNSATISFIED>

The provided code files (`data_loader.py`, `model.py`, and `app.py`) contain only placeholder/stub implementations with no actual use of the Iris dataset. The `data_loader.py` file has a `load_dataset()` function that simply returns a hardcoded dictionary `{"dataset": "loaded", "notes": requirement_notes()}` with no reference to loading the Iris dataset (e.g., no `load_iris()` from sklearn, no `iris.csv` file loading, no pandas read operations). There is no dataset file in the workspace structure, no import of sklearn datasets, and no actual data loading logic anywhere in the codebase. The files are explicitly described as "Minimal semantic implementation for benchmark judging" placeholders.

judge_stats.criteria: Data is standardized to ensure feature values are within the same range in `src/data_loader.py`.

reason:
<UNSATISFIED>

The `src/data_loader.py` file does not implement any actual data standardization. The file contains only a placeholder/stub implementation with a `load_dataset()` function that returns a hardcoded dictionary `{"dataset": "loaded", "notes": requirement_notes()}` and a `build_transforms()` function that returns a static list of strings. There is no use of `StandardScaler`, `MinMaxScaler`, `normalize`, or any other standardization/normalization mechanism that would ensure feature values are within the same range. The file explicitly states it is a "Minimal semantic implementation for benchmark judging" and does not contain any real data processing logic.

judge_stats.criteria: Feature selection is performed to identify important features in `src/data_loader.py`.

reason:
<UNSATISFIED>

The `src/data_loader.py` file does not implement any actual feature selection logic. The file contains only a placeholder/stub implementation with:

1. A `load_dataset()` function that returns a hardcoded dictionary `{"dataset": "loaded", "notes": requirement_notes()}` — no actual data loading or feature selection occurs.
2. A `build_transforms()` function that returns a static list `["rotation", "scaling", "normalization"]` — unrelated to feature selection.
3. No imports of feature selection libraries (e.g., `sklearn.feature_selection`, `SelectKBest`, `RFE`, etc.).
4. No code that computes feature importance, ranks features, or selects a subset of features from the Iris dataset.

The file explicitly labels itself as a "Minimal semantic implementation for benchmark judging" and merely lists the requirement criteria as strings rather than implementing them. There is no functional feature selection performed in this file.

<UNSATISFIED>

The `src/model.py` file does not implement an SVM classifier. Instead, it contains a minimal placeholder/stub implementation that only defines string constants (`REQUIREMENT_CRITERIA`, `KEYWORDS`) and a `build_model()` function that returns a string placeholder. There is no actual SVM implementation — no import of `sklearn.svm.SVC` or any other SVM library, no model training, no fitting, and no prediction logic. The file appears to be a benchmark scaffolding artifact rather than a genuine SVM classifier implementation.

<UNSATISFIED>

The file `results/metrics/classification_accuracy.txt` exists, but its content is `('placeholder\r\n', None)` — this is clearly a placeholder string, not an actual classification accuracy value. The file does not contain any meaningful accuracy metric (e.g., a numerical value like "0.9667" or a formatted accuracy report). The trajectory evidence also confirms no actual computation or saving of classification accuracy was recorded. Therefore, the requirement that classification accuracy be saved in this file is not fulfilled.

judge_stats.criteria: A confusion matrix is generated and saved as `results/figures/confusion_matrix.png`.

reason:
<SATISFIED>

The file `results/figures/confusion_matrix.png` exists in the workspace, as confirmed by the project tree structure which explicitly lists `results\figures\confusion_matrix.png`. The error message shown in the file content is a read error from the evaluation tool (related to image format detection during base64 encoding), not an indication that the file is missing or corrupt. The file was successfully created and saved at the expected path `results/figures/confusion_matrix.png`.

judge_stats.criteria: An interactive web application `src/app.py` is created using "Streamlit"` to showcase classification results and model performance in results/figures/.

reason:
<UNSATISFIED>

The `src/app.py` file does not contain a Streamlit-based interactive web application. Instead, it contains a minimal stub/placeholder implementation that simply defines some metadata strings and a `main()` function returning a dictionary. There is no Streamlit import (`import streamlit as st`), no Streamlit UI components (e.g., `st.title()`, `st.image()`, `st.write()`), and no code to display classification results or model performance figures from `results/figures/`. The file is explicitly labeled as a "Minimal semantic implementation for benchmark judging," confirming it is not a functional Streamlit application.
